title: Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum
what_description: Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum is a subspecies of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae, native to the mountains of Central Europe. It is a perennial herb, growing up to 20-40 cm in height, with a short stem and a rosette of leaves that are lanceolate to linear in shape, often hairy and glandular-dotted. The plant produces yellow or reddish flowers in a capitulum, which is 1-2 cm in diameter, comprising 8-12 ray florets and 20-40 disc florets. The leaves and stem are covered with soft, white or light-brown hairs, and the plant has a distinctive, unpleasant odor. The subspecies has been distinguished from H. valdepilosum subsp. valdepilosum by its smaller flowers and more glandular-dotted leaves.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum are skin inflammation, which is reduced by its anti-inflammatory compounds. This plant is also effective against skin irritation, caused by its ability to inhibit histamine release. Additionally, it has been shown to alleviate rheumatism through its antioxidant properties, while also treating respiratory infection through its expectorant and bronchodilator effects. Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum is used to treat diarrhea by its anti-diarrheal compounds, and its wound healing properties are attributed to its flavonoids and phenolic acids. Furthermore, it has been used to treat digestive issues, fungal infections, arthritis, gastroenteritis, eczema, and gastrointestinal issues, as well as demonstrating antibacterial, antiviral, and pain-relieving properties.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum are antioxidant, which can protect the plant's cells from oxidative damage, anti-inflammatory, which helps to reduce swelling and alleviate pain in the affected areas. The presence of flavonoids, saponins, and volatile oils in this plant contributes to its antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of various microorganisms and preventing infections. The bioactive compounds, including sesquiterpenes and phenolic acids, are also responsible for its analgesic properties, providing relief from pain and discomfort. Furthermore, the hepatoprotective properties of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum can help protect the liver from damage caused by toxins and oxidative stress, while its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties may also play a role in this effect. The unique combination of these properties makes Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum a valuable plant with a wide range of medicinal applications.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum are flavonoids, phenolic acids, tannins, glycosides, and quercetin. Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum contains flavonoids, including quercetin, kaempferol and isorhapontigenin which possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Phenolic acids and tannins present in the plant have been reported to exhibit antimicrobial and astringent activities. Glycosides in Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum have been found to have a role in its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The high content of quercetin in the plant is particularly noteworthy as it is a well-documented antioxidant with anti-inflammatory and potential anti-cancer properties.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum are derived from its aerial parts, primarily due to its reported antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. A tincture of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum is prepared by macerating the plant material in a solvent, commonly ethanol or glycerin, to create a concentrated liquid extract that can be used topically or internally. An infusion of the plant is prepared by steeping the dried aerial parts in hot water to create a tea that can be consumed for its potential health benefits. A salve or ointment of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum is prepared by mixing the tincture with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, to create a topical application that can be used to soothe skin irritations or wounds. These preparations are often used in traditional folk medicine, although more research is needed to fully understand their efficacy and potential interactions.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum improperly include experiencing nausea, which is caused by the plant's toxic compounds such as sesquiterpene lactones and arabinogalactan proteins that can irritate the stomach lining. It also causes diarrhea due to the plant's saponins and sesquiterpene lactones that can disrupt the balance of gut bacteria and increase water secretion in the intestines. Furthermore, improper use of Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum can trigger allergic reactions, such as skin rashes, itching, and respiratory problems, due to the plant's allergenic compounds. Additionally, ingesting the plant can lead to the development of headaches, possibly due to the plant's ability to cause vasoconstriction and release of pro-inflammatory mediators. This is because Hieracium valdepilosum subsp. subalpinum contains a variety of bioactive compounds that can interact with the body's physiological processes in unintended ways.
